title: The Evolution of Translation
concept: Machine translation has evolved from dictionary-based rules to statistical alignment, then to RNN-based Seq2Seq, and finally to pure Transformer architectures.

options.text: Using computers to translate text between languages
A human dictionary book printed in multiple languages
Learning a new language by traveling abroad

exercises.explain: Machine translation is the automated process of translating text from one language to another using computers.

cards.title: Lost in Translation?
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how online translators went from giving hilariously broken sentences to producing near-perfect translations? The secret lies in a major evolution of technology, changing how computers "read" human languages.

cards.title: The Four Eras of Translation
cards.body:
Machine translation has evolved through four major eras:
1. **Rule-Based:** Using literal dictionary lookups.
2. **Statistical:** Matching patterns using math and probability.
3. **Seq2Seq (RNNs):** Processing sentences word-by-word in sequence.
4. **Transformers:** Analyzing entire sentences at once using attention to understand context.

cards.title: The Translation Team
cards.body:
Imagine translating a book. 

A dictionary translation looks up word-by-word, leaving sentences messy. 

A statistical translation looks at how past translators matched phrases, like studying old translation logs. 

A Transformer is like a team of bilingual readers who read the entire page simultaneously, understanding the whole context before writing a single word in the new language.

cards.title: Context is Key
cards.body:
Look at how different eras translate "The bank of the river":
• **Rule-Based:** Might translate "bank" as a financial institution (money bank) because it cannot read the context word "river".
• **Transformer:** Instantly links "bank" with "river" to choose the correct word for a slope of land, ensuring the translation makes perfect sense.

cards.title: More Than a Dictionary
cards.body:
**Myth:** Translation is just swapping words from one language's dictionary to another.
**Fact:** Languages have different sentence structures, idioms, and context. Modern AI translates the *meaning* of a sentence, not just the individual words.

cards.title: Sequential vs. Parallel
cards.body: Compare how old sequential models (RNNs) and modern Transformers read sentences.

left.text: Reads one word at a time in order. Slow and forgets early words in long sentences.

right.text: Sees the whole sentence at once. Easily connects words that are far apart.

exercises.prompt: Which translation era relied heavily on manual dictionary lookups and rigid grammatical rules?
exercises.explain: Rule-based systems relied on fixed dictionaries and grammatical rules, which often made translations sound stiff and unnatural.

exercises.prompt: True or False: RNN-based translation models process the entire sentence at the exact same time.
exercises.explain: RNNs process words step-by-step in order, whereas Transformers process all words at the same time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the sentence about modern translation technology.
exercises.sentence: Modern translation tools rely on the ________ architecture to read sentences all at once.
exercises.explain: The Transformer architecture allows AI to read whole sentences at the same time, replacing the slower word-by-word processing of RNNs.

exercises.prompt: A French idiom "Merde" translates literally to "Shit" but means "Good luck" in theater. Spot the translation generated by a modern AI that understands cultural context:

options.text: Break a leg tonight on the stage!
Good luck with your performance tonight!
Fracture a limb this evening on platform.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an advanced AI (Transformer) because it correctly structures the sentence and translates the idiom. Option A is a literal dictionary translation. Option C is a broken statistical guess.

exercises.prompt: Why are modern Transformer models so much better at translation than older RNN architectures?

options.text: They translate words one by one in a strict sequence
They look at all words at once to capture overall context
They only work with simple dictionary word lookups
They ignore grammar rules entirely to save computer memory

exercises.explain: Transformers process the entire sentence at once, allowing them to capture long-range relationships between words far better than step-by-step RNNs.
